summary: The author achieved 10th place by treating the competition as a direct multi-class classification problem and ensembling 15 QLoRA-finetuned models across three Qwen backbones. Key technical choices included a mixed Focal and Cross-Entropy loss with label smoothing and a 33% weight warmup to stabilize training on long-tail classes, alongside rigorous data hygiene to manage label noise and near-duplicates. Inference was heavily optimized using GPTQ-4bit quantization and vLLM to split and run the 32B model efficiently, followed by a weighted logit blend with Top-3 selection for the final submission.
extensive_summary:
## Overview & Data Strategy
The solution frames the competition as a direct multi-class classification task. The author emphasizes rigorous data hygiene, including auto-fixing True/False inconsistencies and ensuring near-duplicate samples remain within the same validation fold to prevent data leakage. Target construction involved concatenating `Category` and `Misconception` columns, with missing values filled as 'NA'.

## Models & Training
Three backbone architectures were utilized via `AutoModelForSequenceClassification`: Qwen3-Reranker-8B, Qwen3-Embedding-8B, and Qwen2.5-32B-Instruct. Each was trained with 5-fold QLoRA, yielding 15 models total. Training employed a mixed loss of Focal loss (computed on unsmoothed CE) and Cross-Entropy with label smoothing (0.1). To stabilize training on long-tail classes, per-class weights were applied after a `weight_warmup_ratio` of ~0.33. The 8B models used bitsandbytes 4-bit NF4 quantization with gradient checkpointing, while the 32B model was trained on an A800-40GB.

## Inference Engineering
Inference was optimized for memory efficiency. The 8B models kept LoRA adapters unmerged and loaded a 4-bit quantized base. For the 32B model, the author merged LoRA weights, split the base model from the classifier head, and applied GPTQ-4bit quantization (group_size=64, symmetric, GAR, true_sequential=True) to the base while keeping the head in FP16. The quantized base was run through vLLM in `embed` mode with `override_pooler_config={"pooling_type": "LAST", "normalize": False}` to extract embeddings, which were then passed through the external FP16 classifier head to produce logits.

## Ensembling & Results
Final predictions used a weighted logit blend (weight 2 for 32B folds, weight 1 for 8B folds) followed by Top-3 selection, which proved more stable than probability averaging for the MAP@3 objective. The ensemble of 15 models achieved a private MAP@3 of 0.948.
competition_flow: Raw CSV data is cleaned and split into 5 folds with near-duplicates grouped together, then fed into three Qwen backbones fine-tuned via QLoRA with a mixed Focal/CE loss, followed by GPTQ-4bit quantization and vLLM embedding for inference, and finally combined via weighted logit blending and Top-3 selection for submission.
data_reading: NaN filling for the Misconception column
data_processing: Keeping near-duplicates in the same fold
Applying per-class weights with 33% warmup for long-tail classes
cv_strategy: 5-fold cross-validation with near-duplicates kept in the same fold
ensembling: Weighted logit blend (weight 2 for each 32B fold, weight 1 for each 8B fold) followed by Top-3 selection for MAP@3, which proved more stable than probability averaging.
insights: Clean data splits and balanced training with many modest models outperform heavier pipelines for tasks with label noise, long-tail distributions, and MAP@3 objectives.
critical_findings: Using a weighted logit blend with Top-3 selection is more stable than probability averaging for MAP@3.
A 33% weight warmup is critical to stabilize early training updates on long-tail classes.
Keeping near-duplicates in the same validation fold prevents data leakage and improves generalization.
what_did_not_work: Hierarchical multi-task classifier with hard logic constraints
